case_name: Liyakat Ali vs Smt Chunni Devi
citation: FIRST APPEAL FROM ORDER No. - 600 of 2009

compensation_breakdown.notes: Tribunal award upheld; liability kept on tractor owner due to policy breach (non-agricultural use).

facts: On 02.12.2000, Arvind Kumar was going on a scooter near Hyderabad Paisara when Tractor No. U.P. 35-B 1649 took a sharp turn and iron rods loaded in the attached trolley/trailer struck him. Arvind Kumar died on the spot and an FIR was lodged. The tractor was insured but no separate premium/insurance was taken for the trolley, and the MACT, Unnao awarded Rs.1,18,850/- against the tractor owner (appellant). The owner appealed disputing liability, not the quantum.
arguments_claimant: The appellant-owner argued that the trailer/trolley is an attachment and part of the tractor and has no independent propulsion, so it need not be separately insured and the insurer should be liable since the tractor was duly insured. He further asserted that the tractor was being used to transport bricks and iron rods for construction of a room to secure pakki boring equipment for irrigation, hence for agricultural purposes, and that the driver had a valid tractor driving licence. He relied on Nagashetty v. United India Insurance Company Ltd. and Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. J. Kotiratnama.
arguments_respondent: The insurer contended that while the tractor was insured, the trolley was not insured and no premium was paid for it, and therefore the insurer was not liable. It further argued that the policy covered agricultural use only, but the vehicle was used to carry bricks and iron rods, constituting violation of policy terms and conditions.
ratio_decidendi: The Court held that although a trailer/trolley attached to a tractor can be treated as part of the insured tractor so that the insurer cannot avoid liability merely because the trolley was not separately insured, that principle does not apply where there is a policy breach as to permitted use. It stated: "we observed that the trailer/trolley, which is attached with the tractor is a part of the tractor, which was duly insured from the insurance company. Hence, the insurance company cannot escape from its liability to pay compensation by taking the plea that the trailer was not separately insured." However, on the facts it found the tractor was insured "only for the purpose of agricultural activity" and that "Transportation of iron rods and bricks is certainly not an agricultural activity"; therefore "there is a violation of the terms and conditions of the insurance policy." Consequently, "we are of the view that in the instant case, the insurance company is not liable to pay the compensation and the owner of the tractor will have to pay the compensation," and the tribunal award against the owner was upheld.
final_order: Appeal dismissed; the judgment and award dated 27.01.2009 in Claim Petition No. 117 of 2001 awarding Rs.1,18,850/- was upheld (though for different reasons), and the appellant-owner was directed to deposit the balance amount, if any, before MACT, Unnao within two months; registry to transmit the amount to the Tribunal.
summary: This motor accident appeal concerned who must pay compensation for the death of Arvind Kumar caused by iron rods protruding from a tractor-trolley. The owner argued that the insurer should pay because the tractor was insured and the trolley was merely an attachment. The High Court held that an attached trolley may be treated as part of the tractor for insurance purposes, but in this case the tractor was being used to carry bricks and iron rods for construction, which was outside the agricultural-use policy. The court therefore upheld the award of Rs.1,18,850/- against the tractor owner and dismissed the appeal.
embedding_summary: Liyakat Ali vs Smt Chunni Devi, FIRST APPEAL FROM ORDER No. 600 of 2009, decided on 16.07.2010 (Bench: Devi Prasad Singh, Dr. Satish Chandra, J; delivered by Dr. Satish Chandra, J), appeal under Section 173 Motor Vehicles Act 1988 from MACT, Unnao award dated 27.01.2009 in Claim Petition No. 117 of 2001. Accident on 02.12.2000 near Hyderabad Paisara: Tractor No. U.P. 35-B 1649 took sharp turn; iron rods loaded in attached trolley/trailer hit scooter rider Arvind Kumar causing death on the spot; FIR lodged. Tribunal awarded Rs.1,18,850/- against tractor owner because tractor insured but trolley not separately insured; quantum not disputed on appeal, only liability (insurer vs owner). Court discussed definitions of tractor and trailer (National Insurance Company v. Chinnamma, 2004 (3) TAC 577 SC) and held trailer/trolley attached is part of tractor and insurer cannot avoid liability solely for lack of separate trolley insurance; relied also on United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Surinder, 2005 (1) TAC 270 (P&H) and reasoning on trailer insurance (Parsottambhai Kanbhai v. Panchiben alias Ratanben, 1977 ACJ 441). However, because tractor insured for agricultural use only and was carrying bricks/iron rods for construction (policy breach), insurer held not liable; owner to pay. Appeal dismissed; award of Rs.1,18,850/- upheld; owner directed to deposit balance within two months.
legal_principles: Trailer/trolley attached to tractor treated as part of tractor for coverage; insurer cannot avoid liability solely because trolley not separately insured
Distinction between 'trailer' and mechanically propelled 'motor vehicle'—trailer has no independent propulsion and is an attachment to a prime mover
Policy-limited use (agricultural purpose) breach can exonerate insurer from liability where vehicle used for non-agricultural construction transport
When quantum is undisputed, appellate court confines decision to liability (owner vs insurer)
sections_cited: Section 61 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988
cases_cited: National Insurance Company v. Chinnamma and others; 2004 (3) TAC 577 SC
Parsottambhai Kanbhai v. Panchiben alias Ratanben, 1977 A.C.J. 441
United India Insurance Company Ltd. v. Surinder and others; 2005 (1) TAC 270 (P&H)
Nagashetty v. United India Insurance Company Ltd.; 2001 A.C.J. 1441; 2001 (3) T.A.C. 511
Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. J. Kotiratnama, 2000 (3) An. W.R. 91

Arvind Kumar died on the spot and an FIR was lodged. The tractor was insured but no separate premium/insurance was taken for the trolley, and the MACT, Unnao awarded Rs.1,18,850/- against the tractor owner (appellant). The owner appealed disputing liability, not the quantum. The appellant-owner argued that the trailer/trolley is an attachment and part of the tractor and has no independent propulsion, so it need not be separately insured and the insurer should be liable since the tractor was duly insured. He further asserted that the tractor was being used to transport bricks and iron rods for construction of a room to secure pakki boring equipment for irrigation, hence for agricultural purposes, and that the driver had a valid tractor driving licence. He relied on Nagashetty v. United India Insurance Company Ltd. and Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. J. Kotiratnama. The insurer contended that while the tractor was insured, the trolley was not insured and no premium was paid for it, and therefore the insurer was not liable. It further argued that the policy covered agricultural use only, but the vehicle was used to carry bricks and iron rods, constituting violation of policy terms and conditions. The Court held that although a trailer/trolley attached to a tractor can be treated as part of the insured tractor so that the insurer cannot avoid liability merely because the trolley was not separately insured, that principle does not apply where there is a policy breach as to permitted use. It stated: "we observed that the trailer/trolley, which is attached with the tractor is a part of the tractor, which was duly insured from the insurance company. Hence, the insurance company cannot escape from its liability to pay compensation by taking the plea that the trailer was not separately insured." However, on the facts it found the tractor was insured "only for the purpose of agricultural activity" and that "Transportation of iron rods and bricks is certainly not an agricultural activity"; therefore "there is a violation of the terms and conditions of the insurance policy." Consequently, "we are of the view that in the instant case, the insurance company is not liable to pay the compensation and the owner of the tractor will have to pay the compensation," and the tribunal award against the owner was upheld. Appeal dismissed; the judgment and award dated 27.01.2009 in Claim Petition No. 117 of 2001 awarding Rs.1,18,850/- was upheld (though for different reasons), and the appellant-owner was directed to deposit the balance amount, if any, before MACT, Unnao within two months; registry to transmit the amount to the Tribunal. The owner argued that the insurer should pay because the tractor was insured and the trolley was merely an attachment. The High Court held that an attached trolley may be treated as part of the tractor for insurance purposes, but in this case the tractor was being used to carry bricks and iron rods for construction, which was outside the agricultural-use policy. The court therefore upheld the award of Rs.1,18,850/- against the tractor owner and dismissed the appeal.
